mnemonics.title: MITM Defense Checklist
mnemonics.content: Always Verify Identity (AVI): Authentication first, then establish communication. Use Digital certificates, Implement Mutual verification, Trust but Verify every message, Monitor for anomalies.

mnemonics.title: Layers of MITM Defense
mnemonics.content: Defend at Every Layer (DEL): Data Link (ARPGuard), Network (IPsec), Transport (TLS), Application (Certificate Pinning). Each layer catches what others miss.

acronyms.usage: Attack where adversary intercepts and potentially modifies communications between two parties
Framework enabling secure communication through digital certificates and CA verification
Web security policy protecting against SSL stripping attacks
Real-time certificate revocation checking to detect compromised certificates
Trusted entity that issues digital certificates for verifying identities
Maps IP addresses to MAC addresses; target of spoofing in Layer 2 MITM attacks
List of certificates that have been revoked and should not be trusted

visualTips.visualization: Draw three entities in triangle: Alice (left), Oscar (center, larger), Bob (right). Show arrows: Alice→Oscar→Bob with different key icons. Oscar has two key pairs - one for Alice, one for Bob. Each connection uses different colored line showing separate encryption.

Show four circles: Alice's private 'a', Oscar's 'o', Bob's 'b'. Arrows show: Alice sends g^a to Oscar, Oscar sends g^o to Bob, Oscar receives g^b from Bob, Oscar sends g^o to Alice. Final keys: Alice-Oscar = g^ao, Bob-Oscar = g^bo, Alice-Bob = NEVER connected.
Draw HTTPS request going to attacker, who converts to HTTP, server responds with HTTPS, attacker converts to HTTP for victim. Show lock icon being 'stripped' at each conversion point.

analogies.analogy: Like a postal worker who opens all letters, reads them, reseals them, and delivers them. Neither sender nor receiver knows their communication is being intercepted and potentially modified.
Like having a VIP list at a club. Even if someone has a valid ID (certificate) from a government (CA), they can't enter unless they're on the club's specific list (pinned).
Like two strangers trying to agree on a secret password in a crowded room, calling out their guesses publicly. Anyone can hear and intercept, pretending to agree with each separately.
Like intercepting all mail at a building's mailroom before it reaches individual apartments. The mail carrier (network) delivers to the compromised point first.
